judgments.comparative_verdict: Both reports hit all five expectations with essentially equivalent citations and specificity; Report 2 adds slightly more depth (ADR review-flow gate, remediation-adequacy critique, self-contradiction on mitigation) but the core required findings are comparable in quality.

report_1.evidence: Per `incidents/OPS-412-timeline.md`, failures began at **14:03** and the first alert fired at **14:50** — a **47-minute** detection gap.

report_2.evidence: Per `larkpay-comms/incidents/OPS-412-timeline.md`, checkout failures began at 14:03 and the first alert fired at 14:50 — a **47-minute detection gap**.

report_1.evidence: The internal timeline `incidents/OPS-412-timeline.md` records that **1,284 transactions failed and required manual reprocessing, and 37 were unrecoverable and refunded**.

report_2.evidence: The internal timeline `larkpay-comms/incidents/OPS-412-timeline.md` records **1,284 failed transactions requiring manual reprocessing, of which 37 were unrecoverable and refunded**.

report_1.evidence: April 2026 uptime was **99.77%** (`metrics/uptime-2026.csv`), below the contractual **99.9% monthly SLA** defined in `docs/sla-commitments.md`, which says any month below 99.9% "is a breach and owes service credits."

report_2.evidence: `larkpay-comms/docs/sla-commitments.md` sets a contractual **monthly** SLA of 99.9%, with any month below that being a breach that owes credits scaled to its depth. April 2026 was 99.77% (`larkpay-comms/metrics/uptime-2026.csv`), so this incident put the month in breach.

report_1.evidence: Blaming "the on-call engineer" violates style-guide rule 1 and must be removed.** `docs/customer-comms-style-guide.md` rule 1: "Never assign blame to an individual." The sentence "The outage was caused by the on-call engineer deploying an untested config change" does exactly that.

report_2.evidence: Blaming "the on-call engineer" must be removed.** `larkpay-comms/docs/customer-comms-style-guide.md` rule 1: "Never assign blame to an individual." The draft's root-cause sentence ("caused by the on-call engineer deploying an untested config change") violates it directly.

report_1.evidence: The draft must be restructured to lead with customer impact and remediation (style-guide rule 3).** `docs/customer-comms-style-guide.md` rule 3 requires leading with impact and remediation; the draft instead opens with a reliability marketing paragraph ("LarkPay has maintained 99.99% uptime...") and closes with more of the same ("Our engineering culture treats reliability as the first feature...").

report_2.evidence: The document must be restructured to lead with customer impact and remediation.** Style guide rule 3. The draft instead opens with an uptime boast and closes with culture praise ("Our engineering culture treats reliability as the first feature…"), with impact buried and misstated in the middle.
